# Almanac model

The cosine day-length model and its latitude, longitude, accuracy, and dependency contracts.

**Applicability:** Owning domain selectors: `internal/almanac/**`. Topic selectors: `internal/almanac/**`. Both domain and topic selectors must match. Current matched paths: `internal/almanac/almanac.go`, `internal/almanac/almanac_test.go`. Marker sites: `internal/almanac/almanac_test.go:8 [invariant] almanac/model:almanac-clamped-latitude`.

The almanac package approximates sunrise and sunset times with a lightweight cosine model. It
deliberately trades ephemeris-grade precision for a small, instant, dependency-free computation. The
claims below capture the model's current behaviour and its accuracy boundaries.

## Claims

### `rule: cosine-day-length-model`

Daylight duration comes from a cosine model in which the seasonal solar declination drives the
day and night terminator angle. No ephemeris routine or external dependency participates.
Origin: ADR-0001

### `rule: longitude-shifts-solar-noon`

Solar noon shifts by four minutes per degree of longitude away from the reference meridian.
Origin: ADR-0001

### `rule: polar-results-collapse`

Polar day and polar night collapse to a full-length or zero-length day; extreme latitudes never
produce an error.
Origin: ADR-0001

### `rule: minutes-level-accuracy`

The model targets minutes-level accuracy at temperate latitudes and is knowingly wrong near the
poles. Sub-minute precision is out of scope without a successor decision accepting an ephemeris
dependency.
Origin: ADR-0001

### `rule: standard-library-only`

The internal/almanac package depends only on the Go standard library.
Origin: ADR-0001

### `invariant: almanac-clamped-latitude`

Latitude is clamped to the range -90 to 90 before the day-length model runs, so out-of-range input
degrades to the pole rather than leaving the domain of the model and producing an error.
Origin: ADR-0001
Backing: test
